title: Figma Make Gains Voice-to-Text, Agentic Skills & Version History in May 2026 Release
why_it_matters: Figma Make is evolving from a prototype generator into a full agentic design environment, letting teams automate repeated workflows and iterate faster using natural language. The addition of voice-to-text prompts and reusable Skills lowers the barrier for designers to harness AI without leaving their primary tool. For product teams, version history and context resetting finally bring the kind of safety nets that make experimental AI workflows viable in production settings.
whats_happening: In its May 2026 release notes event, Figma shipped a significant update to Figma Make — its AI-powered prototyping and build environment. The update introduced voice-to-text dictation so designers can speak prompts directly into Make chat, question cards that present structured decision points before the AI moves forward, full version history so any prior build state can be instantly restored, and a clear-context option to reset an AI session without losing file work. Alongside these, Figma launched custom Skills — markdown files that encode a team's conventions and workflows — which can be triggered via slash commands and will soon be shareable across organisations. The May release also highlighted agentic workflows via Figma's MCP server, allowing external AI agents to write directly to the Figma canvas using a team's real components, variables, and styles. Live demos at the accompanying release notes livestream showed product teams co-designing with AI agents to take vibe-coded prototypes further, connect design systems to code, and ship faster from the Figma canvas itself.
source_name: Figma Help Center / Figma Release Notes

title: Apple's Liquid Glass Design Language Reaches Third-Party Apps as iOS 26 Adoption Accelerates
why_it_matters: Liquid Glass is being called Apple's most significant UI overhaul since iOS 7, and its cross-platform rollout across iOS 26, iPadOS 26, and macOS Tahoe 26 means designers building in the Apple ecosystem must rethink how they approach layering, depth, translucency, and motion from the ground up. For product teams, any app recompiled against the iOS 26 SDK automatically inherits the new system controls, creating both a consistency opportunity and a risk for apps with heavy custom UI. The debate around legibility and accessibility trade-offs is already shaping how practitioners choose to apply — or limit — the effect.
whats_happening: Apple has been actively promoting its Liquid Glass design system through an updated Design Gallery showcasing how leading third-party apps — including AllTrails, Fantastical, and GoodLinks — have adopted the new material language. Liquid Glass is not a purely visual skin; it is described as a dynamic material system that mimics real glass, featuring translucency, refraction, depth, and motion responsiveness while intelligently adapting to content, light, and interaction. The design philosophy evolved from Apple's visionOS spatial computing work. Rolled out universally across iOS 26, iPadOS 26, macOS Tahoe 26, watchOS 26, and tvOS 26, the system makes tab bars, navigation bars, and toolbars appear to float above content rather than sit flat. iOS 26.5 is currently in beta, and Apple is already shifting focus toward WWDC 2026 on June 8, where iOS 27 is expected to be unveiled. Designer and community reaction remains mixed — praise for the immersive depth sits alongside criticism of legibility issues with overly transparent layers on complex backgrounds.

title: Google Stitch Evolves Into an AI-Native 'Vibe Design' Canvas Challenging Traditional Design Tools
why_it_matters: Google Stitch's transformation into a multi-screen, agent-driven design workspace signals a broader industry shift: the first phase of a design workflow — going from zero to a structured UI concept — is rapidly being automated. By lowering the barrier for non-designers (product managers, founders, developers) to generate production-quality UI in seconds, Stitch is compressing the time between ideation and stakeholder validation. Its DESIGN.md format and Firebase Studio export path also hint at a future where design systems are documented and transferred in agent-friendly, machine-readable formats rather than proprietary file types.
whats_happening: Google Labs evolved Stitch (originally launched in May 2025 after acquiring Galileo AI) into a full AI-native software design canvas in March 2026, and the tool has continued gaining traction throughout May 2026. The platform lets anyone turn natural language, sketches, images, or voice commands into high-fidelity UI designs powered by Gemini AI. The new infinite canvas allows multiple design directions to run in parallel, with a design agent that reasons across the entire project's evolution. A key feature is the DESIGN.md format — an agent-friendly markdown file for exporting and importing design system rules across tools and projects — alongside direct exports to Figma (with proper Auto Layout structure) and Firebase Studio for production handoff. Stitch generates up to five connected screens from a single prompt, automatically mapping user journeys between them. The tool remains free through Google Labs, with 350 standard generations and 50 experimental Gemini 2.5 Pro generations per month at no cost.

title: AI Is Becoming an Ambient Layer Inside UI — Not a Button to Press
why_it_matters: The shift from 'AI as a feature' to 'AI as ambient infrastructure' has major implications for how design teams structure information architecture, interaction models, and trust signals. When AI is invisible by default and surfaces only on demand, designers must rethink the choreography of when, where, and how intelligence appears — avoiding the twin failure modes of over-automation and over-disclosure. This trend also raises the stakes for UX research, since the quality of ambient AI behaviour is deeply dependent on understanding user context, intent, and tolerance for AI error.
whats_happening: A convergent signal is emerging from multiple leading design voices and research organisations in 2026: AI in product interfaces is transitioning from a prominent, button-triggered chatbot model to an embedded, ambient layer that activates only when contextually relevant. Nielsen Norman Group's State of UX 2026 report flagged that trust will be a major design problem for AI experiences this year, with users who have been burned by AI features becoming more hesitant to adopt new ones — putting pressure on designers to deliver transparency, control, consistency, and graceful failure handling. The Tubik design blog similarly identified a key UI trend of 2026 as AI moving away from acting as an all-knowing autopilot toward a more restrained copilot role — present in sidebars, overlays, and collapsible panels rather than hijacking primary flows. Design studio Orizon frames this as 'ambient AI': intelligence that lives quietly inside the UI, reducing cognitive load by shifting the user's focus from talking to an AI to simply getting work done faster. This pattern is visible in Google Gemini's Chrome integration and in the broader product design community's push toward contextual, intent-aware AI interfaces rather than standalone AI chat modules bolted onto existing products.

title: Figma's Agentic MCP Workflows Let Engineers and Designers Co-Build Directly on the Canvas
why_it_matters: The Figma MCP server effectively dissolves a longstanding boundary: design files are no longer artifacts handed from designer to developer, but live workspaces that AI agents and engineers can write to directly using real production components. For design teams, this means design systems become executable inputs to agentic workflows — a significant elevation of their strategic value. For product organisations, it compresses the design-to-code cycle in ways that were previously only possible with bespoke tooling, and opens up the question of how design governance and QA must evolve when agents can modify canonical design files autonomously.
whats_happening: Figma's May 2026 release roundup placed significant emphasis on its Model Context Protocol (MCP) server infrastructure, which allows external AI coding agents (such as those running in Cursor or other agentic environments) to read from and write directly to Figma design files using a team's existing components, variables, and styles. The /figma-use skill — also called 'write to canvas' — comes pre-installed with Figma's MCP server and lets an agent create and modify real Figma design files. Practical workflows showcased at the May release livestream included using an agent to capture a code-side dark mode into Figma design and library screens, and using agents to generate alternative design directions on the canvas from existing user research findings — all without designers starting from scratch. FigJam was also highlighted as a space for engineers and AI agents to collaboratively reason through system architecture, addressing the growing complexity that comes from agent-written code being merged faster than teams can review it. The remote MCP server at mcp.figma.com is now the preferred connection path for organisations, with a desktop MCP server option for enterprise-specific use cases.
